title: Still fit for purpose? Upgrading our economic models for an age of uncertainty
gist: The ECB is overhauling its macro-forecasting toolkit after its models underestimated the 2021-24 inflation surge, shifting from linear, aggregate, representative-agent models to disaggregated energy models (BVAR), state-dependent pass-through, machine-learning density forecasts and heterogeneous-agent (HANK) frameworks, and is now applying non-linear elasticities to live scenarios such as Middle East conflict escalation and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

themes.theme: Why ECB models missed the 2021-24 inflation surge

insights.claim: When the ECB reran its pre-2021 forecasting models using the commodity prices, interest rates and exchange rates that actually materialized, the models were technically valid but still underestimated the 2021-24 inflation surge, showing the shortfall came from mis-specified transmission equations rather than bad input assumptions.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Model risk sat in the propagation equations, not the input forecasts — a warning that a macro model can look valid on reruns yet still miss forward-looking inflation.
insights.quote: our models proved to be valid. However, they still underestimated the inflation surge that followed.
insights.locator: When old tools fail to capture new shocks

insights.claim: Linear macro models understate inflation pass-through because the relationship between energy-price shocks and headline inflation is non-linear: larger initial shocks produce disproportionately stronger pass-through, and the effect is amplified further when inflation is already elevated.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Anyone modeling inflation from a fresh energy shock should expect convexity, not a straight-line extrapolation from historical betas.
insights.quote: the larger the initial shock, the stronger the pass-through of higher energy prices to inflation
insights.locator: When old tools fail to capture new shocks

insights.claim: Second-round effects during 2021-24 were stronger than historical models implied: firms passed higher input costs to consumer prices more aggressively than past patterns suggested, and wages caught up with price increases unusually quickly.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: A wage-price feedback loop can re-emerge faster than legacy models predict once inflation is already high, arguing for closer tracking of real-time wage and pricing survey data over reliance on historical elasticities.
insights.quote: Firms passed higher input costs through to consumer prices more aggressively than historical patterns might have suggested. Meanwhile, wages caught up with higher prices unusually rapidly.
insights.locator: When old tools fail to capture new shocks

insights.claim: The ECB's pre-2021 model equations were estimated largely on data from a low-inflation environment, so they no longer captured the true parameters governing pass-through once inflation surged, implying models trained on one inflation regime can silently mis-price a different regime.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Any model or strategy calibrated on 2010s low-inflation data may be miscalibrated for a higher inflation/volatility regime, arguing for recalibration on higher-inflation-era data.
insights.quote: the equations of our models were no longer able to capture the key parameters of the post-pandemic economy because they were estimated largely on the basis of data drawn from a low-inflation environment
insights.locator: When old tools fail to capture new shocks

themes.theme: War and geopolitics break standard correlations

insights.claim: Following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, oil, gas and electricity prices decoupled from their normal co-movement, illustrating how standard economic correlations embedded in models can break down entirely during wartime.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Cross-commodity correlation assumptions used in risk models should be treated as regime-dependent, not stable, during geopolitical conflict.
insights.quote: oil, gas and electricity prices decoupled unexpectedly following Russia's invasion of Ukraine
insights.locator: In times of war standard correlations can break down

insights.claim: The ECB's supply-constraint satellite models, originally built to explain the 2021 global supply-chain bottlenecks, are now being reused to assess inflation risk from newer shocks, including the 2025 'Liberation Day' blanket US tariffs and the 2026 Gulf energy-export disruptions tied to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The ECB is actively treating 2025 tariff escalation and the 2026 Hormuz disruption as live, comparable supply-shock inflation risks — worth watching euro-area core inflation prints for tariff/Hormuz pass-through.
insights.quote: Examples include the tariff volatility following the blanket imposition of tariffs on US imports in 2025 (on so-called "Liberation Day") or the Gulf energy export disruptions amid the closure of the Strait of Hormuz in 2026.
insights.locator: In times of war standard correlations can break down

themes.theme: Disaggregated energy modeling and new data inputs

insights.claim: The ECB has moved from modeling aggregate energy prices to a disaggregated, bottom-up suite that splits pre-tax energy prices into fuel, gas and electricity subcomponents, using Bayesian Vector Autoregressive (BVAR) models with stochastic volatility and outlier correction to improve short-term inflation forecasting accuracy.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: ECB short-term inflation projections should now be more responsive to fuel-, gas- or electricity-specific shocks individually, rather than reacting to a single blended energy index.
insights.quote: The new models split pre-tax energy prices into subcomponents: fuel, gas and electricity.
insights.locator: In times of war standard correlations can break down

insights.claim: The ECB's new granular energy models are explicitly designed to capture the inflation impact of forthcoming regulatory changes such as the EU Emissions Trading System 2 (ETS 2), which extends carbon pricing to buildings and transport fuel.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: ETS 2 is being explicitly modeled as an inflation driver by the ECB — worth tracking its implementation timing as a euro-area inflation catalyst.
insights.quote: This granular modelling allows us to better gauge the effects of structural changes in energy markets, extreme weather events and climate policies, such as the forthcoming EU Emissions Trading System 2 (ETS 2).
insights.locator: In times of war standard correlations can break down

insights.claim: The ECB now cross-checks its inflation projections with higher-frequency alternative data, including surveys, market-based measures, news-based attention indices, Google search activity and mobility data, aggregated into harmonised weekly activity indices for timelier near-term signals.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Confirms nowcasting-style alternative data (search trends, mobility, news sentiment) is now formally embedded in ECB policy-relevant forecasting rather than a research curiosity — relevant for anticipating the ECB's reaction function in real time.
insights.quote: drawing on surveys, market-based measures, news-based attention indices, Google search activity and mobility data
insights.locator: In times of war standard correlations can break down

insights.claim: The ECB re-estimated its core projection models, including ECB-BASE and the New Area-Wide Model (NAWM II), using a longer historical sample, with the most attention going to two channels: monetary policy transmission and commodity price shock pass-through.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Recalibrated transmission channels could change how quickly ECB rate moves are expected to feed into inflation and growth forecasts going forward.
insights.locator: Focus on core projection models

insights.claim: The re-estimated NAWM II now includes a direct oil-price propagation channel that separates the pass-through of commodity price shocks from other foreign price shocks, which were previously bundled together.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: A dedicated oil channel lets ECB models isolate oil-driven inflation from other imported-price effects, useful for decomposing future euro-area inflation surprises.
insights.quote: the New Area-Wide-Model (NAWM) II now includes a direct oil price propagation channel to separate the pass-through of commodity price shocks from other foreign price shocks
insights.locator: Focus on core projection models

insights.claim: Firms change prices more frequently when inflation is already high, rather than absorbing cost increases as they did in calmer periods, meaning the Phillips curve relationship between inflation and its drivers is not fixed but shifts across inflation regimes.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Implies inflation is likely to be more persistent and pass-through-heavy in any future high-inflation episode, since firms are conditioned to reprice faster once inflation expectations are unanchored.
insights.quote: The Phillips curve, in other words, is not a fixed object.
insights.locator: Focus on core projection models

insights.claim: The ECB has adopted machine learning methods, including quantile regression forest models, to produce density forecasts of inflation that do not impose a predetermined functional relationship between inflation and its drivers, making them more useful for gauging tail risks around the baseline forecast.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Signals a structural shift toward data-driven, non-parametric risk assessment at the ECB, implying more emphasis on inflation risk distributions than single-point forecasts in future communications.
insights.quote: machine learning approaches that do not impose a specific predetermined relationship between inflation and its drivers
insights.locator: Focus on core projection models

insights.claim: During the pandemic, government spending and transfers combined with the ECB's long-term refinancing operations and quantitative easing to cushion aggregate demand, while in 2022 monetary tightening was paired with government measures shielding households and firms from higher energy prices; the ECB's new models can now assess the macroeconomic and distributional impact of this fiscal-monetary interaction.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The new models let the ECB explicitly quantify how fiscal energy-shielding measures interact with and can offset monetary tightening, relevant for gauging the true effective policy stance beyond the headline rate.
insights.locator: Beside foreign shocks, public policy interventions

insights.claim: A forthcoming ECB framework called DREAM, a flexible multi-country model, is being built to enable integrated, structural policy assessment across the open-economy, heterogeneous euro-area monetary union.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Watch for DREAM's rollout as a new ECB lens for assessing asymmetric shock impacts across individual euro-area member states.
insights.locator: footnote on DREAM framework

themes.theme: Heterogeneous households and monetary policy transmission

insights.claim: The ECB is moving beyond time-invariant two-agent (TANK) models toward Heterogeneous Agent New Keynesian (HANK) models that characterize the full joint wealth and income distribution of households, enabled by recent advances in deep learning and Sequence Space Jacobian computational methods that make large-scale HANK estimation feasible.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: HANK adoption lets the ECB assess distributional winners and losers of policy, relevant for understanding political-economy constraints on future rate decisions.
insights.locator: Wealth and income matter for how households react to shocks

insights.claim: Heterogeneous agent models let the ECB study how monetary policy affects households differently depending on income, wealth and asset composition, and how monetary and fiscal policy can be jointly designed in response to shocks such as rising energy prices.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Distributional analysis could inform more targeted fiscal offsets alongside ECB tightening, reducing political backlash risk during future energy shocks.
insights.locator: Wealth and income matter for how households react to shocks

themes.theme: New scenario methodology and quantified non-linearity

insights.claim: In the ECB's March 2026 staff projections, facing the prospect of Middle East conflict escalation, the ECB did not simply scale up a baseline pass-through coefficient for the scenario but instead adjusted core model elasticities based on scenario severity, reflecting empirical evidence that inflation pass-through is state-dependent and strongest when shocks are large and the economy is already running hot with high inflation.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The ECB's own scenario work assumes convex, not linear, inflation risk from a Middle East escalation — investors pricing euro-area inflation/rate paths under such a scenario should expect asymmetric upside risk rather than a simple linear extrapolation.
insights.quote: the exercise went beyond simply scaling up a baseline pass-through coefficient. Instead, the core model elasticities were adjusted in line with the severity of the scenario.
insights.locator: To conclude

insights.claim: According to ECB estimates, a 10% rise in HICP energy and food prices raises HICP core inflation by 0.09% under a linear model, but by between 0.12% and 0.22% under the ECB's new non-linear model, depending on the level of prevailing inflation and how tight the labor market is.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: A concrete, quantified range showing a 33%-144% larger core-inflation response than the old linear estimate — a directly usable input for forecasting the ECB's reaction to the next energy price spike.
insights.quote: a 10% rise in HICP energy and food raises HICP core by 0.09% according to the linear model, and by between 0.12% and 0.22% in the non-linear model, depending on the state of the economy.
insights.locator: Chart 1 note

insights.claim: The ECB acknowledges its modeling toolkit still has analytical gaps and is developing new models to evaluate structural long-term issues combined with macro-financial linkages, including defence spending, competitiveness policy, geopolitical developments, artificial intelligence, demographic change and long-term fiscal challenges.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: A preview of what may show up in future ECB scenario analyses — defence spending, AI diffusion and demographic shifts are being framed as structural macro variables worth modeling explicitly.
insights.quote: This will enable analyses of the macroeconomic effects of such key issues as defence spending, competitiveness policies, geopolitical developments, artificial intelligence, demographic change and long-term fiscal challenges.
insights.locator: However, some analytical gaps still persist
